description: This chart plots density (unit: kg/m³) on the y-axis, comparing three datasets for alloying elements relevant to ferritic/low alloy steels: apparent density of ferrite (calculated as the inverse of regression coefficients from Model II, represented by horizontal bars with 95% probability error bars), pure elemental density (square markers), and average literature-reported apparent density (triangle markers) for elements including Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, Mo, Ni, S, Si, V. The underlying regression model for ferrite density has an R² of 0.994, maximum deviation of 4.2 kg/m³, and average deviation of 1.4 kg/m³. Close agreement between regression results and reference values is found for Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, and S, with minor deviation for Mo, and larger deviations for Ni, Si, V. The results confirm that Cu and Mo increase ferrite density, while C, Cr, Mn, Ni, S, Si, and V reduce ferrite density, supporting the reliability of the developed density prediction model for ferritic steels.

This is a scatter plot illustrating the density of cementite with Pnma and Pbnm crystal structures as a function of Cr and Mn mass content respectively. The x-axis represents the mass content of alloying elements, ranging from 0 to 1, while the y-axis represents density in Kg/m³, ranging from 7000 to 7800 Kg/m³. Diamond markers represent density values of Cr-alloyed cementite, and triangle markers represent density values of Mn-alloyed cementite. Both Cr and Mn additions monotonically reduce cementite density: as Cr content increases from 0 to ~0.22, cementite density decreases from ~7750 Kg/m³ to ~7580 Kg/m³; as Mn content increases from 0 to ~0.95, cementite density drops from ~7700 Kg/m³ to ~7080 Kg/m³. This figure provides experimental basis for the non-linear effect of Cr and Mn on cementite density, which is incorporated into the thermodynamic density prediction model (Model II) for ferritic/low alloy steels to improve prediction accuracy.

This chart presents regression results for austenite apparent density (defined as 1/regression coefficient r_i) alongside pure elemental/phase density values and literature average apparent density data for components relevant to austenitic steels, with the y-axis representing density in kg/m³ ranging from 0 to 12000 kg/m³. Three datasets are visualized: regression-derived apparent density (solid horizontal bars with error ranges), pure element/phase density (open square markers), and literature average apparent density (open triangle markers). Covered elements and phases include C, Co, Cr, Cu, fcc Fe, Mn, Mo, N, Ni, Si, Ti, and TiC. The plotted data confirms the study's finding that solutes TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, Ti reduce austenite density, while Co, Cu, Mo, Ni increase austenite density. The corresponding austenite density regression model shows high prediction accuracy, with R²=0.975, maximum deviation 10.4 kg/m³, and average deviation 2.6 kg/m³.
